G3.fix_sketch:
Fail closed, identically in both languages, when an occurrence-mode scope aggregate leaves [i64::MIN, u64::MAX], reusing the existing error string "Aggregate sample values exceed the supported integer range." routed through the standard validation-error envelope (exit 2). Python (clankerprof/scopes.py, in the per-frame boundary loop around line 485): after each `boundary_stats.total_time += value` (the max-magnitude accumulator; category/leaf/owner accumulators are bounded by it per scope), check `if not (AGGREGATE_MIN <= boundary_stats.total_time <= AGGREGATE_MAX): raise ValueError(AGGREGATE_BOUNDS_ERROR)` importing the shared constants from clankerprof.facts/model so the message stays byte-identical.

Rust (crates/clankerprof-core/src/scopes.rs): make the accumulation function return Result; after each `total_time += value` check against model::AGGREGATE_MIN/AGGREGATE_MAX and return Err(model::AGGREGATE_BOUNDS_ERROR.to_string()). Optionally also replace the panicking `json!(...)` sites for TimeNs aggregates (e.g. render_metrics_object at scopes.rs:704) with a checked i128->Value helper as defense in depth, so out-of-range can never panic during serialization.

Docs (docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md ~129-136 and the scopes contract ~317-321): amend the bound statement — the import-time bound guarantees subset sums only; occurrence-mode scopes attribute a sample once per occurrence, so both implementations additionally enforce the same bound during scope accumulation and fail with the identical validation error rather than emitting out-of-range integers or panicking. Also mirror the note on model.rs check_aggregate_bounds's doc comment. Add a Python/Rust parity test pinning identical error envelopes on this fixture (reuse .goalloop-support/triage2/G3/facts.json shape) plus an in-range recursive-scope case pinning identical byte output.

Rejected alternatives: serde_json arbitrary_precision in Rust (crate-wide number-formatting blast radius, and Python output would still violate the documented envelope); clamping/float fallback (violates the "exact" promise); tightening the import bound by max stack depth (rejects currently-valid profiles that never use occurrence scopes).

G13.fix_sketch:
Rust-only fix (Python is the correct reference; its output is unchanged, so byte parity is restored by making Rust match Python's declaration-order semantics):

1. crates/clankerprof-core/Cargo.toml: `toml = { version = "0.8", features = ["preserve_order"] }` — without this, toml::Table is itself BTreeMap-backed and order is already lost before any JSON conversion. (serde_yaml 0.9's Mapping already preserves document order.)

2. crates/clankerprof-core/src/scopes.rs: stop round-tripping configs through plain `serde_json::Value`. Do NOT just enable serde_json's `preserve_order` feature — that flips every serde_json::Map in the crate from key-sorted to insertion-ordered, including all emitted report JSON that currently comes out key-sorted (and byte-identical to Python's sort_keys) for free. Instead, introduce a small order-preserving config tree used only by config loading, e.g.:
   enum ConfigValue { Null, Bool(bool), Int(i64), Float(f64), Str(String), Seq(Vec<ConfigValue>), Map(Vec<(String, ConfigValue)>) }  // or IndexMap<String, ConfigValue>; indexmap is already a dependency
   with lossless `From<serde_yaml::Value>` and `From<toml::Value>` conversions. Change `config_to_json` to return ConfigValue and port its consumers (`load_boundary_options`, `aliased_config_value`, `load_boundary_categories`, `load_boundary_domains`, `load_boundaries`, `predicate_expr`, `string_values`) to it. Iterating `Map(Vec<..>)` then yields declaration order, so first-match attribution matches Python for both [cost_kind] and [owner] (and any other order-sensitive table such as [scope.rollup]).

3. Regression tests: add an order-sensitivity case to the parity suite (tests/test_clankerprof_rust_parity.py) — two overlapping cost-kind definitions with alphabetically inverted names (ZFirst before ASecond) plus an analogous overlapping [owner] pair, in both YAML and TOML, pinned byte-for-byte; plus a Rust unit test asserting load_boundary_categories preserves declaration order.

Docs: add the explicit sentence to docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md (scope contract section, near lines 288-312): "Configured cost-kind and owner definitions are evaluated in declaration order; the first matching definition wins."
